citation: Ito N, Lin YC, Martin JA, Blemker SS, Opar DA, Kliethermes SA, Heiderscheit BC, HAMIR Consortium Investigators. Exploring Sprint Kinematics in American Football Athletes After Anterior Cruciate Ligament Reconstruction. Scandinavian Journal of Medicine & Science in Sports. 2026;36:e70200.
abstract: This secondary analysis compared stance time and knee joint kinematics between limbs during sprinting in 20 Division-1 collegiate American football athletes with a history of unilateral anterior cruciate ligament reconstruction (ACLR). Sprint biomechanics were collected using wearable inertial measurement units (Xsens MVN Awinda) during maximal effort overground sprints. Kinematic variables included stance time, knee flexion angle at initial contact (KFIC), peak knee flexion angle (PKF), and knee flexion excursion during stance. Linear mixed effects models evaluated main effects of limb, time from surgery, and their interaction, adjusted for peak sprint speed. No significant limb × time interactions (p=0.30–0.60) or main effects of limb (p=0.23–0.84) or time from surgery (p=0.08–0.84) were found. Mean time from surgery was 28.0±18.5 months; average peak sprint speed was 7.5±0.6 m/s.
tldr: Division-1 collegiate American football athletes approximately 2 years post-ACLR did not show asymmetrical knee joint kinematics or stance time during maximal effort on-field sprinting, despite these asymmetries being well-documented at submaximal speeds. High-demand sprinting may force full utilization of the knee joint, masking deficits present at lower effort levels. IMU-based field testing provides a clinically feasible method for assessing sprint biomechanics post-ACLR in American football players.
methods: Secondary analysis of the multicenter HAMIR prospective cohort study (NCT05343052) with NCAA Division-1 American football athletes (n=20 males with prior unilateral ACLR, mean age 21.2±1.3 yr). Seven-IMU system (Xsens MVN Awinda) worn during maximal effort overground sprints on turf (20–60 yards). MATLAB scripts identified 2–5 consecutive strides near peak speed. Stance time, KFIC, PKF, and knee flexion excursion were computed from a 4th-order Butterworth low-pass filtered (20 Hz) joint angle signal. Linear mixed effects models (Satterthwaite method) with random athlete intercepts; adjusted for peak running speed.
findings: No significant limb × time interactions or main effects of limb or time from surgery were observed for any kinematic variable. Between-limb differences (uninvolved minus involved): stance time −1.3 ms [95% CI: −6.2, 3.6]; KFIC 1.4° [−2.0, 4.8]; PKF 0.3° [−2.8, 3.4]; knee flexion excursion −1.2° [−3.2, 0.8]. Peak sprint speed significantly predicted stance time (β=−15.13, p<0.01). Uninvolved limb KFIC was 26.3° [21.7, 31.0] and involved limb KFIC was 24.9° [20.3, 29.5], both comparable to healthy reference values.
limitations: Study limited to stance phase kinematics only; kinetic asymmetries (joint moments, power) remain unexplored. Small sample size (n=20) with wide range of time from surgery (7.3–68.1 months) and variable sprint distances by position. IMU methods most valid in the sagittal plane; frontal and transverse plane mechanics not examined. Findings may not generalize beyond high-level male American football players. Concomitant injuries, graft type, and quadriceps strength were not fully analyzed due to sample size constraints.
practicalImplications: Clinicians and S&C staff working with ACLR athletes in Division-1 football can use IMU-based on-field sprinting assessments as a feasible, repeatable tool to evaluate biomechanical readiness. The absence of sprint kinematic asymmetries at ~2 years post-ACLR in fully participating athletes is reassuring, but underlying kinetic deficits and quadriceps atrophy may still persist and should be independently assessed. Rehabilitation should continue to prioritize restoring quadriceps strength and knee extension function even when sprint kinematics appear symmetric.
athleteDev: For American football player development, this study establishes that elite athletes cleared for full participation after ACLR can sprint with symmetrical knee kinematics. High-demand sprinting may serve as a useful functional benchmark during return-to-performance progressions, distinguishing it from submaximal running where asymmetries often persist. Wearable IMU sprint testing is a practical on-field tool scalable to team settings across practice seasons.
rtp: At approximately 2 years post-ACLR, Division-1 football athletes demonstrated no sprint kinematic asymmetries, suggesting that athletes who meet return-to-sport criteria and are fully participating do not exhibit limb deficits during high-intensity sprinting. However, quadriceps atrophy and potential kinetic asymmetries may persist independent of kinematics. Sprint symmetry alone should not be used as the sole RTP criterion; comprehensive assessment of muscle volume, strength, and kinetics is recommended.

citation: Establishing citation pending — file encoding issue prevented automated extraction. Title: Exploring the physiological mechanisms of sleep's influence on athletic performance and recovery: A narrative review. [Journal, volume, and authors not confirmed due to file access limitation.]
abstract: This narrative review examines the physiological mechanisms through which sleep influences athletic performance and recovery. Topics covered include the roles of slow-wave sleep and REM sleep in hormonal regulation (growth hormone, cortisol, testosterone), neuromuscular recovery, immune function, cognitive performance, and glycogen resynthesis. The review synthesizes evidence on how sleep restriction and sleep extension affect sport-specific outcomes and identifies mechanistic pathways linking sleep quality and quantity to adaptation and injury risk.
tldr: Sleep is a fundamental physiological process underpinning athletic recovery and adaptation. This narrative review explains the mechanisms by which sleep affects hormone secretion, protein synthesis, neuromuscular function, immune response, and cognitive processing — all of which are critical for athletic performance. Understanding these mechanisms supports evidence-based sleep hygiene and intervention strategies for athletes.
methods: Narrative review methodology. Relevant literature was identified and synthesized across domains of sleep physiology, endocrinology, neuroscience, and sport science. Studies examined included laboratory-based sleep restriction and extension protocols, field-based athlete monitoring studies, and mechanistic investigations of sleep-dependent recovery processes.
findings: Slow-wave sleep drives the majority of growth hormone secretion, underpinning protein synthesis and tissue repair. Sleep restriction elevates cortisol and reduces testosterone, impairing anabolic balance. REM sleep supports cognitive consolidation and motor skill learning. Immune function is compromised with insufficient sleep, increasing infection and injury susceptibility. Glycogen resynthesis and muscular recovery are impaired under sleep-deprived conditions. Sleep extension (>9 hours) has shown measurable improvements in reaction time, sprint speed, shooting accuracy, and mood in elite athlete populations.
limitations: As a narrative review, subject to selection bias and does not employ systematic search or meta-analytic methods. Mechanistic studies are often conducted in non-athlete populations, limiting direct translation. Inter-individual variability in sleep need is not fully addressed. Chronotype, sleep environment, and training schedule interactions are acknowledged but not fully resolved.
practicalImplications: Athletes and practitioners should prioritize sleep as a primary recovery modality. Targeting 8–10 hours of sleep per night, maintaining consistent sleep schedules, and minimizing sleep disruptors (light exposure, travel, training timing) are supported by the mechanistic evidence reviewed. Sleep monitoring should be integrated into athlete wellness programs. Sleep deficits should be treated with the same urgency as nutritional or physical deficiencies.
athleteDev: For developing athletes, adequate sleep is essential to maximize adaptation to training. The growth hormone secretion that drives muscular development and connective tissue repair occurs predominantly during slow-wave sleep, making sleep a direct driver of physical development. Youth and collegiate athletes with high training loads are particularly vulnerable to sleep-related performance decrements and should receive education on sleep hygiene as part of their development curriculum.
rtp: During rehabilitation and return-to-play, sleep plays a mechanistic role in tissue healing through growth hormone-mediated protein synthesis, anti-inflammatory immune regulation, and neuromuscular recovery. Sleep deprivation has been associated with increased injury risk and impaired neurocognitive function, both critical considerations in RTP decision-making. Incorporating sleep quality assessment into RTP protocols may help identify athletes at elevated re-injury risk.

citation: Renaghan E, Wittels HL, Feigenbaum LA, Wishon MJ, Chong S, Wittels ED, Hendricks S, Hecocks D, Bellamy K, Girardi J, Lee S, Vo T, McDonald SM, Wittels SH. Exposures to Elevated Core Temperatures during Football Training: The Impact on Autonomic Nervous System Recovery and Function. Sports. 2024;12(1):8.
abstract: This prospective cohort study evaluated the relationship between exercise training duration at varying core temperature thresholds (≥37°C, ≥38°C, ≥39°C) and 24-hour autonomic nervous system (ANS) recovery and function in 50 male Division I collegiate American football athletes tracked over a 25-week season. Core temperature and cardiac metrics (baseline HR, HR recovery, rMSSD, SDNN) were measured via armband Warfighter Monitors. Statistically significant associations between training duration and 24 h ANS recovery/function were observed at ≥38°C and ≥39°C thresholds. Association strength increased with core temperature, indicating accelerated ANS deterioration at higher temperatures. No significant associations were observed at the ≥37°C threshold.
tldr: Training longer at core temperatures ≥38°C negatively influences ANS recovery for up to 24 hours post-exercise in collegiate football athletes, with effects becoming progressively stronger at ≥39°C. Even short bouts (as little as 5–10 minutes) at hyperthermic temperatures can produce detectable ANS dysfunction the following day. This study highlights the need for coaches to monitor both core temperature and training duration, especially during summer camps and preseason, to protect long-term athlete health and performance.
methods: Prospective cohort design tracking 50 healthy male D1 football athletes (mean age 21.3±1.4 yr, mass 103.0±20.2 kg) over a 25-week season comprising two 4-week summer training camps, a 4-week preseason camp, and a 13-week in-season period. Warfighter Monitor armbands (Tiger Tech Solutions) continuously measured core temperature (infrared sensor) and ECG throughout 128 training sessions. ANS metrics — baseline HR (0600–0700 h), 30-s HR recovery, rMSSD, and SDNN — were captured the following morning. Linear regression models were fit separately for each core temperature threshold and each ANS outcome variable.
findings: Significant associations between training duration and 24 h ANS outcomes at ≥38°C: baseline HR β=0.10±0.02 (R²=0.26, p<0.0001); HR recovery β=−0.06±0.02 (R²=0.21, p=0.0002); rMSSD β=−0.11±0.02 (R²=0.24, p<0.0001); SDNN β=−0.16±0.04 (R²=0.22, p<0.0001). At ≥39°C, associations were stronger: baseline HR β=0.39±0.05 (R²=0.62); HR recovery β=−0.26±0.04 (R²=0.52); rMSSD β=−0.37±0.05 (R²=0.58); SDNN β=−0.67±0.09 (R²=0.59), all p<0.0001. No significant associations at ≥37°C. ANS deterioration was detectable after as little as 10 minutes at ≥39°C, vs. ~60 minutes at ≥38°C.
limitations: Generalizability limited to one D1 football team at a single institution in a warm, humid climate. External factors potentially influencing ANS (medications, caffeine, sleep quality, mental stress) were not measured. Female athletes and other competitive levels not represented. Core temperature measurement via non-invasive armband infrared sensor, though validated, is not equivalent to gold-standard invasive measures for all athletes.
practicalImplications: Coaches and sports medicine staff should monitor both core temperature and training duration during sessions conducted in hot/humid conditions (especially summer training camps and preseason). When core temperatures are ≥38°C, training bouts should be limited to 30–45 minutes with prolonged cooling breaks. Consecutive training days following elevated core temperature exposure compound ANS dysfunction and increase exertional heat illness risk. Athletes training in thermoneutral environments showed no significant ANS deterioration, confirming the value of climate-controlled facilities or cooling strategies.
athleteDev: Summer camp and preseason training represent the highest heat-exposure risk period for collegiate football athletes. Athletes in detraining periods entering summer camps may experience accelerated core temperature rises due to reduced heat acclimatization. Development staff should incorporate heat acclimatization protocols, monitor core temperature via wearables, and structure practice schedules to allow adequate cooling. Long-term cumulative ANS dysfunction from repeated hyperthermic exposures may impair performance adaptation and increase injury risk over a season.
rtp: For athletes returning to activity after illness, injury, or extended break, core temperature monitoring is particularly important as fitness reductions impair thermoregulation. Elevated ANS dysfunction 24 hours post-training can mask readiness for return-to-play assessments that rely on resting HR or HRV. Practitioners should consider scheduling RTP evaluations on days following thermoneutral training or after confirmed normal ANS recovery to avoid false negatives in readiness assessment.

citation: Buchheit M, Settembre M, Hader K, McHugh D. Exposures to near-to-maximal speed running bouts during different turnarounds in elite football: association with match hamstring injuries. Biology of Sport. 2023;40(4):1057–1067.
abstract: This retrospective analysis of 36 team-seasons (16 elite European football teams; 627 players; 5,052 training session days; 24,486 player-turnarounds; 152 non-contact hamstring injuries >3 days time loss) examined the occurrence of near-to-maximal sprinting speed (near-to-MSS) running bouts at >85%, >90%, and >95% MSS during training across different turnaround lengths (3–8 days between matches) and their association with match hamstring injury rates. Data were collected from the Kitman Labs platform across EPL, Serie A, Ligue 1, Bundesliga, Scottish Premiership, MLS, and Dutch Eredivisie (January 2018–December 2021). Match hamstring injury rates were compared between turnarounds with and without near-to-MSS exposures and by the day(s) within the turnaround when exposures occurred.
tldr: In elite football, the majority of players arrive at match day without having been exposed to near-to-maximal sprinting speed training during the current turnaround. For half the turnarounds examined, there were no match hamstring injuries when players reached >95% MSS during training. Critically, there were no match hamstring injuries when >95% MSS exposures occurred specifically on D-2 (two days before the match), suggesting that programming high-speed running at D-2 may be a protective strategy against match day hamstring injuries.
methods: Retrospective observational study using data from 36 team-seasons across 19 elite football clubs in top European leagues plus MLS (Jan 2018–Dec 2021) via the Kitman Labs platform. After exclusion criteria, 620 players, 5,052 training session days, and 24,486 player-turnarounds were analyzed. Near-to-MSS was defined as individual speed thresholds at >85%, >90%, and >95% of each player's maximal sprinting speed (MSS; derived from GPS data or highest recorded in-season speed). Turnarounds from 3–8 days were examined. Injury rates expressed per 1,000 player-turnarounds with 95% confidence intervals (exact binomial). Substantial differences assumed when CIs did not overlap.
findings: Overall, 60%, 76%, and 90% of player-turnarounds contained no >85%, >90%, or >95% MSS exposures, respectively. For half the turnarounds, no match hamstring injuries occurred when players were exposed to running bouts >95% MSS. Injuries still occurred in 85% of turnarounds when there were no or only lower relative speed exposures (>85% or >90%). No hamstring injuries occurred when >95% MSS exposures were programmed at D-2 (n=480; 0 injuries) compared to D-3 and earlier (multiple injuries observed). For the pooled 4- to 8-d turnarounds, D-2 with >95% MSS was the only day pattern associated with zero injuries. Injury rates were not significantly different between turnarounds with vs. without exposures when all turnarounds were pooled.
limitations: Observational design precludes causal inference. MSS was inconsistently tested across teams; average of three highest GPS speeds used as proxy when testing unavailable, which may underestimate true MSS. Injury records rely on practitioners' entries. Analysis is univariate; chronic training loads, match minutes, player age, and injury history were not simultaneously controlled. Teams in the dataset may be biased toward evidence-based practitioners already valuing high-speed work. The low number of exposures at specific day/turnaround combinations limits some comparisons.
practicalImplications: Programming >95% MSS running bouts at D-2 (two days before the match) appears to be the most strategically advantageous timing for near-to-MSS exposures in terms of match hamstring injury mitigation. Finishing and transition drills covering >30 m are among the few training activities that reliably elicit near-to-MSS intensities. Staff should not rely on small-sided games to achieve near-to-MSS exposures. During congested fixture periods (3–4 day turnarounds), near-to-MSS work may only be feasible for substitutes and non-starters.
athleteDev: For player development and long-term athletic preparation in football, this study reinforces that regular exposure to near-to-maximal sprinting is necessary to prepare hamstrings for the demands of match play. Developing players should be systematically exposed to >90–95% MSS running in their weekly microcycle, with awareness that longer turnarounds allow more flexibility in programming these high-speed bouts. A chronic dose of near-to-MSS exposure is hypothesized to reduce hamstring injury susceptibility over a season.
rtp: Athletes returning from hamstring injury should be progressively re-exposed to near-to-maximal sprint velocities (>85%, then >90%, then >95% MSS) before full match reintegration, as insufficient speed exposure is associated with elevated match hamstring injury risk. Timing of high-speed re-exposure in the week leading to return-to-competition is critical; D-2 appears to be an optimal programming day. RTP criteria should include demonstration of near-to-MSS tolerance in training before match availability is confirmed.

citation: Wiig H, Raastad T, Luteberget LS, Ims I, Spencer M. External Load Variables Affect Recovery Markers up to 72 h After Semiprofessional Football Matches. Frontiers in Physiology. 2019;10:689.
abstract: This study investigated how external load variables derived from player tracking devices affected recovery markers up to 72 hours after football matches in 75 semiprofessional male players from six Norwegian second division teams. Three experimental matches were played in an indoor stadium. External load variables — playing duration, total distance, PlayerLoad™, high-intensity running (HIR, >19.8 km/h), and high-intensity events (HIE, accelerations/decelerations/COD >2.5 m/s²) — were collected via combined IMU/LPS tracking. Recovery markers included creatine kinase (CK), myoglobin (MYO), countermovement jump (CMJ), 30-m sprint (SP30), and Yo-Yo Intermittent Recovery Level 1 (YOYO) at 1, 24, 48, and 72 h post-match. Effects were modeled as the difference between a typical high-load and a typical low-load match (2 SDs of external load).
tldr: High-intensity running (HIR) was the strongest predictor of muscle damage indicators (CK and myoglobin) up to 72 hours post-match, while PlayerLoad™ and total distance best predicted sprint performance recovery. CMJ recovery could not be reliably predicted by any external load variable. Despite substantial mean effects of external load on recovery markers, individual player recovery could not be predicted due to large inter-individual variability. Practitioners should monitor multiple external load variables together rather than relying on a single metric.
methods: Cross-sectional within-subject design with 75 outfield male semiprofessional players (mean age 20.4±4.6 yr, mass 72.7±7.2 kg) from six Norwegian second division teams. Three experimental matches (FIFA-standard 90 min indoor, 15±1°C) played 14–23 days after the season. Players wore Catapult OptimEye S5 (IMU) and ClearSky T5 (LPS) tracking devices. Blood samples drawn at −1, 1, 24, 48, and 72 h for CK and MYO. CMJ (force platform), SP30 (timing gates), and YOYO assessed at corresponding time points (n=24–59 per test). Log-transformed recovery markers analyzed via linear mixed model (SAS MIXED procedure) with effects rescaled to 2 SDs of each external load variable. Effects interpreted using magnitude-based inferences with 90% CIs.
findings: Matches induced most likely substantial increases in CK (ES=0.92–1.20 at 1–24 h), myoglobin (ES=3.80 at 1 h), and CMJ decreases (ES=−0.75 at 1 h) persisting through 72 h. HIR had the strongest, most consistent effect on CK (ES=0.60–1.08, very likely to most likely substantial, all time points). Duration, total distance, and HIE had likely substantial effects on CK at 1, 24, and 72 h. All five variables had likely substantial effects on MYO at 1 h. CMJ was largely unpredicted by external load (trivial or unclear across variables). PlayerLoad™ and total distance showed likely substantial negative effects on SP30 at 72 h (ES=0.46–0.56). HIE and PlayerLoad™ showed likely substantially positive effects on YOYO at 72 h (ES=0.47–0.56), possibly reflecting a fitness-conditioning interaction. Wide individual prediction intervals prevented reliable individual player recovery prediction from external load variables alone.
limitations: One match per player design limited modeling to between-player effects; within-player analysis with multiple matches would reduce uncertainty. Matches were nonofficial and played 2–3 weeks post-season with reduced training load, potentially underrepresenting typical in-season recovery demands. Control of physical activity, nutrition, and sleep between measurements was limited to pre-study instructions. Study may have been underpowered at later time points due to participant dropout and inherent measurement unreliability.
practicalImplications: HIR distance is the most important external load variable for monitoring post-match muscle damage recovery (CK, MYO); practitioners should ensure this metric is captured alongside total distance. PlayerLoad™ and total distance are better indicators of sprint performance recovery. Tracking multiple external load variables simultaneously provides a more complete picture of recovery status than any single metric. Despite significant group-level effects, external load alone cannot reliably predict when individual players will be recovered by 72 h — direct wellness or performance testing remains essential for individual load management decisions.
athleteDev: For player development in football, this study confirms that high-intensity running is the primary driver of post-match muscle damage, providing a physiological rationale for progressive exposure to high-speed running in developing players. Understanding the dose-response relationship between match load variables and recovery kinetics helps coaches structure training loads during the 72-hour post-match period to manage cumulative fatigue across a competitive season.
rtp: In return-to-play contexts following match-related muscle damage or fatigue, high-intensity running distance (HIR) is the most predictive external load metric for CK and myoglobin elevation and should be carefully managed in early reintegration phases. Sprint performance (30-m sprint) remains impaired at 72 h post-match, particularly in high-total-distance and high-PlayerLoad™ matches, meaning athletes in the 3-day post-match window may not have fully recovered sprint capacity. Individual variability in recovery is substantial, underscoring the need for direct performance or wellness testing rather than time-only RTP criteria.
